math/nan.name: nan
math/nan.description: A floating-point “not a number” (NaN) value. Equivalent to the output of float('nan'). Due to the requirements of the IEEE-754 standard, math.nan and float('nan') are not considered to equal to any other numeric value, including themselves. To check whether a number is a NaN, use the isnan() function to test for NaNs instead of is or ==. Example: >>> import math >>> math.nan == math.nan False >>> float('nan') == float('nan') False >>> math.isnan(math.nan) True >>> math.isnan(float('nan')) Tru...

math/nextafter.name: nextafter
math/nextafter.description: Return the floating-point value steps steps after x towards y. If x is equal to y, return y, unless steps is zero. Examples: math.nextafter(x, math.inf) goes up: towards positive infinity. math.nextafter(x, -math.inf) goes down: towards minus infinity. math.nextafter(x, 0.0) goes towards zero. math.nextafter(x, math.copysign(math.inf, x)) goes away from zero. See also math.ulp(). Added in version 3.9. Changed in version 3.12: Added the steps argument.
